questions.question_text: What type of foreclosure process does Maryland use?
questions.explanation: Maryland's primary foreclosure method is non-judicial power of sale under Real Property §7-105, exercised through the deed of trust's power-of-sale clause. The lender files an Order to Docket and the court ratifies the sale, so the process is sometimes called 'quasi-judicial', but it does not require a full judicial foreclosure lawsuit.

questions.question_text: A Maryland lender intends to foreclose on a delinquent borrower. How many days before filing must the lender send a Notice of Intent to Foreclose?
questions.explanation: Maryland law requires lenders to send a Notice of Intent to Foreclose at least 45 days before filing foreclosure papers with the court. This gives borrowers time to explore alternatives.

questions.question_text: A Maryland borrower receives a foreclosure notice and wants to explore alternatives. Which program may the borrower request?
questions.explanation: Maryland offers a foreclosure mediation program that borrowers may request. Mediation provides an opportunity for the borrower and lender to negotiate alternatives to foreclosure, such as loan modifications.

questions.question_text: After a Maryland foreclosure sale is conducted, what additional step must occur before the sale is final?
distractors: Buyer must record the deed within 10 days
questions.explanation: After the trustee's foreclosure sale, the circuit court must ratify (approve) the sale before it becomes final (Md. Rule 14-305). This post-sale court supervision is why Maryland's power-of-sale process is often described as 'quasi-judicial.'

questions.question_text: A typical Maryland residential foreclosure under a deed of trust containing a power of sale begins with which court filing?
questions.explanation: Foreclosures under a power of sale or assent to decree — the overwhelming majority in Maryland — begin with the filing of an Order to Docket in the circuit court; a complaint to foreclose is used only in the rare case where the instrument contains neither clause. The lender must have already sent the Notice of Intent to Foreclose at least 45 days before filing (RP §7-105.1(c)) and may not file until at least 90 days after default (§7-105.1(b)).

questions.question_text: A Maryland borrower facing foreclosure wants to keep their home. Up to what point can the borrower exercise the right of redemption?
questions.correct_answer: Before the court ratifies the sale
distractors: Up to one year after the sale
At any time before the deed records
questions.explanation: In Maryland, a borrower may redeem the property by paying the full debt at any time before the court ratifies the foreclosure sale. Once the court ratifies the sale, the right of redemption is extinguished.

questions.question_text: A Maryland foreclosure sale yields $280,000 but the outstanding debt is $320,000. Can the lender pursue the borrower for the $40,000 difference?
distractors: Only if the loan was non-recourse
questions.explanation: Maryland permits deficiency judgments, meaning lenders can pursue borrowers for the difference between the foreclosure sale price and the outstanding debt. This applies to both residential and commercial properties.

questions.question_text: A Maryland court ratifies a foreclosure sale on Tuesday. On Wednesday, the borrower attempts to pay the full outstanding debt. Can the borrower still redeem?
distractors: Yes, if they pay court costs too
questions.explanation: Once the Maryland court ratifies a foreclosure sale, the borrower's right of redemption is permanently extinguished. The borrower cannot redeem the property after ratification, regardless of ability to pay.

questions.question_text: To exercise the right of redemption in Maryland, the borrower must pay:
distractors: Fair market value of the property
questions.explanation: Maryland's right of redemption requires the borrower to pay the full outstanding debt, including principal, interest, fees, and costs. Paying only the delinquent amount is not sufficient to redeem.

questions.question_text: A Maryland foreclosure sale produces excess proceeds after satisfying the debt. Who receives the surplus funds?
distractors: The court keeps them as fees
The purchasing bidder receives a refund
questions.explanation: When a Maryland foreclosure sale brings more than the debt, the surplus — after the costs of sale and any junior lienholders are paid — belongs to the former borrower. If the sale falls short, the lender may instead seek a deficiency judgment by motion within 3 years after ratification of the auditor's report (Md. Rule 14-216(b)).

questions.question_text: A Maryland deed of trust involves three parties. Who holds legal title to the property as security for the loan?
questions.explanation: In a deed of trust, the trustee holds legal title as security. The three parties are the borrower (trustor), the lender (beneficiary), and the trustee. On default, the trustee may sell the property under the power of sale, subject to court ratification.

questions.question_text: Which financing instrument is more commonly used in Maryland real estate transactions?
questions.explanation: Both mortgages and deeds of trust are used in Maryland, but deeds of trust are the standard instrument for residential loans. Either instrument may contain a power of sale, which is exercised through Maryland's court-supervised (quasi-judicial) foreclosure process — an Order to Docket is filed and the court ratifies the sale.

questions.question_text: A lender holds a deed of trust on a Maryland home with a power of sale clause. How will the foreclosure be carried out?
questions.correct_answer: Sale under the power of sale, subject to court ratification
distractors: A full judicial lawsuit ending in a foreclosure judgment before sale
A sheriff's sale with no court involvement at all
questions.explanation: Real Property §7-105 authorizes power of sale (and assent to decree) foreclosures. The lender files an Order to Docket in the circuit court, but no judgment is required before the sale — a trustee sells the property under the power of sale, and the court then ratifies the sale. Because of this court supervision, Maryland's process is often called 'quasi-judicial.'

questions.question_text: In a Maryland deed of trust, the lender is known as the:
questions.explanation: In a deed of trust, the lender is the beneficiary who benefits from the security arrangement. The borrower is the trustor (who creates the trust), and the trustee holds legal title as security.

questions.question_text: How does Maryland's power-of-sale foreclosure differ from the process in many other deed-of-trust states?
questions.correct_answer: The court must ratify the sale before it is final
distractors: A judicial judgment is required before the sale
The borrower gets a one-year statutory redemption period after the sale
questions.explanation: Like most deed-of-trust states, Maryland allows power of sale foreclosure (RP §7-105) without a pre-sale judgment. What sets Maryland apart is court supervision: the case is opened by filing an Order to Docket, and the sale is not final until the circuit court ratifies it — a 'quasi-judicial' process. Maryland has no post-sale statutory redemption, and deficiency judgments are permitted.
